### 1. The Hard Doomer

::: aside
**META:** Entry point for the debate. Most LW readers know this view. Make it feel precise, not caricatured. Lead with the argument structure, arrive at the person.
:::

*The first true failure may also be the last. Don't build it.*

**What this group believes:** The central danger is a sufficiently capable AI system that pursues goals not aligned with human welfare and acquires enough influence to prevent correction before anyone can stop it. What makes this view distinctive isn't just the high concern — it's the conjunction: strong optimization pressure, no reliable warning shots, no realistic recovery path, and an alignment problem that is very unlikely to be solved under current conditions. Several things have to go badly at once. This cluster believes they will.

**The cluster includes voices with different entry points:**

- **Eliezer Yudkowsky** grounds this in the formal properties of optimization. His central thesis rests on [instrumental convergence](https://www.lesswrong.com/tag/instrumental-convergence): a sufficiently capable goal-directed system will reflexively develop subgoals like self-preservation and resource acquisition. Consequently, value alignment is not a default outcome, but an extraordinarily brittle mathematical challenge. His [2022 post "AGI Ruin: A List of Lethalities"](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/uMQ3cqWDPHhjtiesc/agi-ruin-a-list-of-lethalities) remains the sharpest articulation of why that alignment math fails by default.
  
  Crucially, Yudkowsky frames this failure as a definitive tipping point—analogous to a nuclear reactor going critical rather than an airplane crashing during a test flight. You cannot iteratively test an unaligned superintelligence, because the first true failure is terminal for the species. If *anyone* successfully builds it, the catastrophic outcome occurs globally. This specific mechanical premise—the inability to safely survive a single mistake—is the exact logic that drives his controversial demand for a global development moratorium enforced by physical military airstrikes if necessary.^[Yudkowsky, ["Pausing AI Developments Isn't Enough. We Need to Shut It All Down"](https://time.com/6266923/ai-pause-dangerous-letter-response/) — TIME, Mar 2023]

- **Nate Soares** occupies the adjacent position with less geopolitical urgency and more formal focus: the inner alignment problem is structurally hard in ways that don't yield to more compute or better prompting. The [MIRI 2024 strategy update](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/xofpLpFjkMFgYX6JC/miri-2024-mission-and-strategy-update) reflects their shared assessment that humanity is not on track.

| | |
|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Unipolar optimizer: a single sufficiently capable system achieves decisive strategic advantage before corrective action is possible |
| **Epistemological stance** | Prior-dominant: the argument rests on structural properties of optimization, not extrapolation from current AI behavior. Showing that GPT-4 doesn't scheme is near-zero evidence against this position. |
| **What they reject** | Warning shots as actionable; architectural avoidance as meaningful risk reduction; tractable alignment research closing the gap in time; coordination as a realistic substitute for solving alignment |
| **What would update them** | In principle: a genuine theoretical resolution of the inner alignment problem; convincing formal evidence that instrumental convergence is weak in the relevant system class; a credible physical enforcement mechanism for a global moratorium. In practice, Yudkowsky's own public record suggests the update bar is set so high as to be effectively unfalsifiable under any near-term evidence. He has been explicit about this: the argument rests on the structure of optimization, not on observations about current systems. That's worth noting as a fact about the cluster, not as a criticism of it. |

**The defamiliarizing observation:** This view is often treated as an emotional or cultural position — the "AI doomer" label does work that obscures the technical argument. The actual case rests on specific claims about optimization, goal stability, and the difficulty of value loading that can and should be engaged with directly. Dismissing it as a personality type rather than an argument cluster is exactly the kind of elision this post is designed to resist.
